subject: Chemistry
q: The increasing basicity order of the following compounds is:
question: The increasing basicity order of the following compounds is:
solution:
In case of polar protic solvents, two factors decide the basic character. The positive inductive effect from alkyl groups which increases basicity.

3o>2o>1o Amine

& the Steric Hindrance. 1o>2 >3o  

Thus, the resultant basic character is determined by taking the above two factors into account.

So order will be 2o>1o>3o if methyl group is present as alkyl group otherwise order will be 2°>3°>1°.

In aromatic & Aliphatic: Aliphatic Amine are more basic than aromatic because the lone pair of N is involved in delocalization.

So basic character is in the order 2o>1o>3o>Aromatic Amine

So (B) > (A) > (C) > (D)

subject: Chemistry
q: The correct stability order of the following diazonium salts is
question: The correct stability order of the following diazonium salts is
solution:
The stability of benzenediazonium salts is governed by the electronic effects of the substituents present on the benzene ring.
The diazonium group $-N_2^+$ is strongly electron-withdrawing. Electron-donating groups (EDG) stabilize the diazonium cation by dispersing the positive charge through resonance or inductive effects, whereas electron-withdrawing groups (EWG) destabilize it by increasing the positive charge density.
In (A), the $-OCH_3$ group at the para position is a strong electron-donating group via the $+M$ effect, which significantly stabilizes the diazonium ion.
In (C), there is no substituent (hydrogen), providing a baseline stability.
In (D), the $-CN$ group is an electron-withdrawing group via $-M$ and $-I$ effects, which destabilizes the cation.
In (B), the $-NO_2$ group is a very strong electron-withdrawing group via $-M$ and $-I$ effects, even stronger than $-CN$, leading to the least stability.
Comparing the effects: $+M$ of $-OCH_3$ (A) > $H$ (C) > $-M$ of $-CN$ (D) > $-M$ of $-NO_2$ (B).
Therefore, the correct stability order is $A > C > D > B$.

subject: Chemistry
q: ' A ' is a neutral organic compound ($\mathrm{M}. \mathrm{F}: \mathrm{C}_{8} \mathrm{H}_{9} \mathrm{ON}$). On treatment with aqueous $\mathrm{Br}_{2} / \mathrm{HO}^{(-)}$, ' A ' forms a compound ' B ' which is soluble in dilute acid. ' B ' on treatment with aqueous $\mathrm{NaNO}_{2} / \mathrm{HCl}\left(0-5^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$ produces a compound ' C ' which on treatment with $\mathrm{CuCN} / \mathrm{NaCN}$ produces ' D '. Hydrolysis of ' D ' produces ' E ' which is also obtainable from the hydrolysis of ' A '. ' E ' on treatment with acidified $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ produces ' $F$ '. ' $F$ ' contains two different types of hydrogen atoms. The structure of ' A ' is
question: ' A ' is a neutral organic compound ($\mathrm{M}. \mathrm{F}: \mathrm{C}_{8} \mathrm{H}_{9} \mathrm{ON}$). On treatment with aqueous $\mathrm{Br}_{2} / \mathrm{HO}^{(-)}$, ' A ' forms a compound ' B ' which is soluble in dilute acid. ' B ' on treatment with aqueous $\mathrm{NaNO}_{2} / \mathrm{HCl}\left(0-5^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$ produces a compound ' C ' which on treatment with $\mathrm{CuCN} / \mathrm{NaCN}$ produces ' D '. Hydrolysis of ' D ' produces ' E ' which is also obtainable from the hydrolysis of ' A '. ' E ' on treatment with acidified $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ produces ' $F$ '. ' $F$ ' contains two different types of hydrogen atoms. The structure of ' A ' is
solution:
Compound A undergoes bromination with aqueous Br₂/HO⁻ to form B, which is soluble in dilute acid, indicating an amino group is present.
B undergoes diazotization with NaNO₂/HCl at 0-5°C to form diazonium compound C.
The diazonium compound is then treated with CaCN/NaOH to form nitrile compound D.
Hydrolysis of D produces carboxylic acid E.
When A is treated with acidified KMnO₄, it produces F containing two different types of hydrogen atoms.

This reaction sequence is characteristic of anthranilic acid (2-aminobenzoic acid), where the amino group directs bromination to the para position, and subsequent reactions proceed through the diazonium intermediate.

The structure shown in option (3), which is 2-aminobenzoic acid, fits all these transformations.

subject: Chemistry
q: Consider the following organic reaction sequence. Choose the final product $(X)$ from the following (consider the major product in all intermediate reactions)
question: Consider the following organic reaction sequence. Choose the final product $(X)$ from the following (consider the major product in all intermediate reactions)
solution:
The starting material is propanoic acid ($CH_3CH_2COOH$).

In step (i), propanoic acid reacts with $NH_3$ upon heating to form propanamide.
$CH_3CH_2COOH \xrightarrow{NH_3, \Delta} CH_3CH_2CONH_2$

In step (ii), propanamide undergoes Hoffmann bromamide degradation with $NaOH$ and $Br_2$ to form ethylamine.
$CH_3CH_2CONH_2 \xrightarrow{NaOH, Br_2} CH_3CH_2NH_2$

In step (iii), ethylamine reacts with nitrous acid ($HNO_2$) to form an unstable diazonium salt, which hydrolyzes to give ethanol.
$CH_3CH_2NH_2 \xrightarrow{HNO_2, H_2O} CH_3CH_2OH$

In step (iv), ethanol reacts with benzenediazonium chloride ($C_6H_5N_2Cl$). Ethanol reduces the diazonium salt to benzene, while getting oxidized to ethanal.
$CH_3CH_2OH + C_6H_5N_2Cl \rightarrow C_6H_6 + CH_3CHO + N_2 + HCl$

The final major product (X) is benzene.

Answer: Benzene

subject: Chemistry
q:
Which statements are True?
A. In Hoffmann bromamide degradation, $4$ moles of NaOH and $2$ moles of Br$_2$ are consumed per mole of an amide
B. Hoffmann bromamide reaction is not given by alkyl amides.
C. Primary amines can be synthesized by Hoffmann bromamide degradation.
D. Secondary amide on reaction with Br$_2$ and NaOH will give secondary amine.
E. The by-products of Hoffmann degradation are Na$_2$CO$_3$, NaBr and H$_2$O.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Which statements are True?
A. In Hoffmann bromamide degradation, $4$ moles of NaOH and $2$ moles of Br$_2$ are consumed per mole of an amide
B. Hoffmann bromamide reaction is not given by alkyl amides.
C. Primary amines can be synthesized by Hoffmann bromamide degradation.
D. Secondary amide on reaction with Br$_2$ and NaOH will give secondary amine.
E. The by-products of Hoffmann degradation are Na$_2$CO$_3$, NaBr and H$_2$O.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
The balanced chemical equation for Hoffmann bromamide degradation is:

$\text{R-CONH}_2 + \text{Br}_2 + 4\text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{R-NH}_2 + \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 + 2\text{NaBr} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$

Statement A is false because $1$ mole of $\text{Br}_2$ and $4$ moles of $\text{NaOH}$ are consumed per mole of amide.

Statement B is false because primary alkyl amides readily undergo this reaction.

Statement C is true as the primary product of this reaction is a primary amine.

Statement D is false because the reaction is strictly given by primary amides ($\text{R-CONH}_2$), not secondary amides.

Statement E is true as the by-products formed are $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$, $\text{NaBr}$, and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$.

Therefore, only statements C and E are true.

Answer: C and E only

options: (i)  <math><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>   (ii)  <math><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msub></math>
solution:
When cyano benzene reacts with hydrochloric acid and water to form benzoic acid which in turn reacts with sodium borohydride to form benzyl alcohol.

Thus benzy lamine is not formed in option (b). Thus using  HCl/H2O and NaBH4 is not a correct method of the preparation of benzyl amine from cyano benzene.

subject: Chemistry
q:
A hydrocarbon ' $\mathrm{P}^{\prime}\left(\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{8}\right)$ on reaction with HCl gives an optically active compound ' Q ' $\left(\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{9} \mathrm{Cl}\right)$ which on reaction with one mole of ammonia gives compound ' $\mathrm{R}^{\prime}\left(\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{11} \mathrm{~N}\right)$. ' $\mathrm{R}^{\prime}$ on diazotization followed by hydrolysis gives ' S '.
Identify $\mathrm{P}, \mathrm{Q}, \mathrm{R}$ and S.
question:
A hydrocarbon ' $\mathrm{P}^{\prime}\left(\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{8}\right)$ on reaction with HCl gives an optically active compound ' Q ' $\left(\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{9} \mathrm{Cl}\right)$ which on reaction with one mole of ammonia gives compound ' $\mathrm{R}^{\prime}\left(\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{11} \mathrm{~N}\right)$. ' $\mathrm{R}^{\prime}$ on diazotization followed by hydrolysis gives ' S '.
Identify $\mathrm{P}, \mathrm{Q}, \mathrm{R}$ and S.
solution:
For Q ($C_4H_9Cl$) to be optically active, it must have a chiral center.
P ($C_4H_8$) = but-2-ene ($CH_3-CH=CH-CH_3$)
P + HCl → Q: $CH_3-CH=CH-CH_3 + HCl \rightarrow CH_3-CH(Cl)-CH_2-CH_3$ (2-chlorobutane, chiral at C-2)
Q + $NH_3$ → R: 2-chlorobutane + $NH_3 \rightarrow CH_3-CH(NH_2)-CH_2-CH_3$ (2-aminobutane)
R → diazotization → hydrolysis → S: 2-aminobutane $\xrightarrow{NaNO_2/HCl}$ diazonium salt $\xrightarrow{H_2O}$ $CH_3-CH(OH)-CH_2-CH_3$ (butan-2-ol)

subject: Chemistry
q: Which among the following is the strongest Bronsted base?
question: Which among the following is the strongest Bronsted base?
solution:
Cyclic amines are more basic than open chain amines of same degree. Because open chain amines undergo umbrella inversion which decreases the basic nature. Hence, among the given compounds, quinuclidine is strongest base.

And also quinuclidine is more basic and more nucleophilic than triethylamine, because of lesser steric hindrance in quinuclidine than in triethylamine. Lesser steric hindrance in quinuclidine is due to all the alkyl groups are “tucked” back and Nitrogen is more exposed and can easily donate its lone pair electrons.

q: \(\mathrm{X} \mathrm{g}\) of ethylamine is subjected to reaction with \(\mathrm{NaNO}_2 / \mathrm{HCl}\) followed by water; evolved dinitrogen gas which occupied \(2.24 \mathrm{~L}\) volume at STP. \(\mathrm{X}\) is \(\qquad\) \(\times 10^{-1} \mathrm{~g}\).
question: \(\mathrm{X} \mathrm{g}\) of ethylamine is subjected to reaction with \(\mathrm{NaNO}_2 / \mathrm{HCl}\) followed by water; evolved dinitrogen gas which occupied \(2.24 \mathrm{~L}\) volume at STP. \(\mathrm{X}\) is \(\qquad\) \(\times 10^{-1} \mathrm{~g}\).
solution:
$\underset{\substack{\text { Mol.wt.45g }}}{\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{NH}_2} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{NaNO}_2+\mathrm{HCl}} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}} \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{OH}+\underset{14 \mathrm{~g}}{\mathrm{~N}_2}$
given : $\mathrm{N}_2$ evolved is 2.24 L i.e. 0.1 mole.
i.e. $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{NH}_2$ (ethyl amine) will be $4.5 \mathrm{~g}$
$(=0.1 \mathrm{~mole})$
Hence the answer $=45 \times 10^{-1} \mathrm{~g}$

subject: Chemistry
q: The increasing order of basicity of the following compounds is :
question: The increasing order of basicity of the following compounds is :
solution: The basic nature of amines can be explained on the basis hybridisation. The basic nature order is sp3>sp2>sp. If the lone pair on nitrogen involved in resonance, then it is less basic than others. In the molecules A, B and D, the nitrogen is sp2 hybridised. In the pyrrole molecule the lone pair on nitrogen involving in conjugation to get aromaticity. Hence, C is most basic and B is least basic.
